title: Rabbit winter management for outdoor colonies
subtitle: Protecting outdoor rabbits from wind, damp, and cold through the winter months.
excerpt: Outdoor rabbits can tolerate cold temperatures well, but wet and wind together are dangerous. Winter management focuses on dry bedding, unfrozen water, and distinguishing cold-induced torpor from genuine illness.
sourceNotes: RWAF winter care guidance. RSPCA outdoor rabbit welfare advice. British Rabbit Council husbandry notes.

glossaryTerms.definition: A state of reduced activity and lowered body temperature in response to cold. Rabbits can become very still and less responsive in very cold weather. Unlike true hibernation, torpor rabbits can be roused. If a rabbit cannot be roused or is cold to the touch, this is an emergency.

Bedding that has absorbed moisture from urine, rain, or condensation. Damp bedding rapidly loses insulating properties and creates a cold, wet environment that chills rabbits and promotes ammonia and bacterial growth.

content.text: Healthy adult rabbits cope well with temperatures down to around 5 C as long as they are dry and out of the wind. Kittens, elderly rabbits, and sick animals need more protection. The priority in winter is managing moisture and draught, not just temperature.
Wind and rain protection

Position hutches and runs with their backs to the prevailing wind. In the UK, this is usually the south-west. Cover open run sides with tarpaulin or clear corrugated plastic sheeting. Leave one side partially open for ventilation. A fully sealed enclosure builds up ammonia faster in winter, as rabbits spend more time indoors.
Raise hutches off the ground on legs or blocks. Cold rises from the ground and a low hutch placed directly on soil or concrete will be significantly colder than one raised 20 to 30 cm.
Avoiding damp bedding
Damp bedding

is the main winter welfare risk. Check the sleeping area every morning. Remove wet patches immediately. In winter, rabbits urinate more in the sleeping area because the run is used less. This means bedding needs changing more often than in summer, not less.

Use large amounts of meadow hay as bedding. Hay retains warmth even when slightly damp and can be topped up easily. Avoid wood shavings in the sleeping box in winter. They compact quickly and lose insulating value when wet.
Water sources in freezing conditions

Rabbit water bottles freeze before the temperature drops below zero. A bottle that has frozen overnight may look normal but have an ice plug preventing water from flowing. Check water provision twice daily in cold weather.

To stop bottles freezing: wrap them in bubble wrap or a knitted sleeve, use a wide ceramic bowl instead (bowls freeze more slowly), or swap in a warm second bottle each evening. Do not add salt or antifreeze to the water.
Dehydration in winter is as serious as in summer. Rabbits who cannot access water stop eating hay, and reduced hay intake leads quickly to GI stasis.
Torpor versus illness in cold weather
Rabbits can enter a state of
torpor
in very cold conditions. A torpid rabbit is still and slow but warm to the touch and can be gently roused. It will begin to move when brought into a warmer space.
Look for these signs of illness in a cold rabbit:
Cold to the touch (ears, paws, belly are cold, not just cool).
No response when gently stroked or spoken to.
Breathing is very shallow or irregular.
Still unresponsive after 15 minutes in a warm room.
If a rabbit does not respond to gentle handling, call the vet at once. Keep it warm on the way. Use a covered carrier.
